front: Which amino acid is the only one that is achiral (no chiral center), and why?
back: Glycine (Gly, G). Its side chain is a single H atom, so the alpha carbon bears two H substituents (plus NH2 and COOH) and is not a stereocenter.

front: Name the two amino acids with sulfur-containing side chains and their codes.
back: Cysteine (Cys, C) and Methionine (Met, M). Cys thiols form disulfide bonds; Met is the start (initiator) amino acid, as N-formyl-Met in prokaryotes and plain Met in eukaryotes.

front: Which 3 standard amino acids are aromatic (have a ring system)?
back: Phenylalanine (Phe, F), Tyrosine (Tyr, Y), Tryptophan (Trp, W). Trp has an indole ring. Trp and Tyr absorb UV ~280 nm (basis of A280); Phe absorbs lower (~257 nm) and contributes little.

front: Which two standard amino acids are acidic (negatively charged side chains at physiological pH)?
back: Aspartate (Asp, D) and Glutamate (Glu, E). Their carboxylic acid side chains (pKa ~4) are deprotonated to COO- at pH 7.4.

front: Which three standard amino acids are basic, and which are positively charged at physiological pH 7.4?
back: Lysine (Lys, K), Arginine (Arg, R), Histidine (His, H). Lys and Arg are protonated/positive at pH 7.4; His (pKa ~6) is mostly neutral (deprotonated) at 7.4.

front: Why is histidine uniquely suited to act as a buffer/catalyst in enzyme active sites at physiological pH?
back: Its imidazole side chain has a pKa ~6, near physiological pH, so it can readily accept or donate protons (exists in both protonated and neutral forms in cells).

front: Which amino acid is an imino acid with a secondary amine, and what structural effect does it have?
back: Proline (Pro, P). Its side chain loops back to the backbone N (cyclic), making it rigid; it disrupts alpha helices and is a frequent helix breaker.

front: List the 9 essential amino acids (must be obtained from diet).
back: Phenylalanine, Valine, Threonine, Tryptophan, Isoleucine, Methionine, Histidine, Leucine, Lysine. Mnemonic: PVT TIM HaLL.

front: What are the approximate pKa values of the alpha-carboxyl and alpha-amino groups of a free amino acid?
back: Alpha-carboxyl (COOH) pKa ~2; alpha-amino (NH3+) pKa ~9-10. At physiological pH the molecule is a zwitterion (COO-, NH3+).

front: What are the approximate side-chain pKa values for aspartate/glutamate, histidine, cysteine, lysine, and arginine?
back: Asp/Glu ~4; His ~6; Cys ~8; Lys ~10.5; Arg ~12.5. (Tyr -OH ~10.)

front: Define the isoelectric point (pI) of an amino acid.
back: The pH at which the molecule carries no net charge (exists as a zwitterion). At this pH it will not migrate in an electric field (electrophoresis).

front: How do you calculate the pI of an amino acid with no ionizable side chain?
back: Average the two pKa values flanking the neutral species: pI = (pKa1 + pKa2)/2, i.e., the alpha-COOH and alpha-NH3+ pKa values.

front: How do you find the pI of an amino acid with an ionizable side chain (e.g., acidic or basic)?
back: Average the two pKa values on either side of the neutral (zwitterionic) form, including the side-chain pKa. Acidic AAs -> low pI (~3); basic AAs -> high pI (~10).

front: What type of bond links amino acids in a protein, and what reaction forms it?
back: A peptide (amide) bond between the alpha-carboxyl of one and the alpha-amino of the next, formed by dehydration (condensation), releasing water.

front: Why does the peptide bond have partial double-bond character, and what is the consequence?
back: Resonance delocalizes electrons between the C=O and C-N, making the bond planar and rigid (no free rotation). It is usually in the trans configuration.

front: What defines the PRIMARY structure of a protein?
back: The linear sequence of amino acids joined by peptide bonds, read N-terminus to C-terminus. Encoded by the gene; no folding involved.

front: What defines SECONDARY structure, and what stabilizes it?
back: Local folding into alpha-helices and beta-pleated sheets, stabilized by hydrogen bonds between backbone amide N-H and carbonyl O atoms.

front: What defines TERTIARY structure, and what interactions stabilize it?
back: Overall 3D shape of a single polypeptide. Stabilized by side-chain interactions: hydrophobic effect (major), disulfide bonds, H-bonds, ionic (salt) bridges.

front: What defines QUATERNARY structure? Give a classic example.
back: Assembly of two or more polypeptide subunits into a functional protein. Example: hemoglobin (2 alpha + 2 beta subunits).

front: What is denaturation, which structural level(s) does it disrupt, and name two causes.
back: Loss of 3D structure (secondary/tertiary/quaternary) with loss of function; primary sequence stays intact. Caused by heat, pH extremes, urea, detergents, or heavy metals.

front: Which enzyme class catalyzes the transfer of a functional group (e.g., phosphate, amino) from one molecule to another?
back: Transferases. Example: kinases transfer phosphate from ATP. (The 6 classes: Oxidoreductases, Transferases, Hydrolases, Lyases, Isomerases, Ligases.)

front: What does the Michaelis constant Km represent, and what does a low Km indicate?
back: Km = substrate concentration at half Vmax. Low Km = high affinity (enzyme reaches half-max speed at low [S]).

front: Write the Michaelis-Menten equation relating velocity to substrate concentration.
back: v = Vmax[S] / (Km + [S])

front: On a Lineweaver-Burk (double-reciprocal) plot, what do the x-intercept, y-intercept, and slope equal?
back: x-intercept = -1/Km; y-intercept = 1/Vmax; slope = Km/Vmax.

front: How does a competitive inhibitor affect Km and Vmax?
back: Increases apparent Km (lower apparent affinity); Vmax unchanged (can be overcome by adding more substrate). Inhibitor binds the active site.

front: How does a pure noncompetitive inhibitor affect Km and Vmax?
back: Km unchanged; Vmax decreased. Binds an allosteric site on E and ES equally; cannot be overcome by adding more substrate.

front: How does an uncompetitive inhibitor affect Km and Vmax?
back: Both Km and Vmax decrease (Km/Vmax ratio constant, so parallel Lineweaver-Burk lines). Binds only to the ES complex.

front: What distinguishes mixed inhibition from pure noncompetitive inhibition?
back: Mixed: inhibitor binds E and ES with different affinities, so Vmax decreases AND Km changes (up or down). Noncompetitive is the special case where affinities are equal (Km unchanged).

front: What is allosteric regulation, and what shape is the velocity-vs-[S] curve for cooperative enzymes?
back: Regulation via effector binding at a site other than the active site, altering activity. Cooperative enzymes give a sigmoidal (S-shaped) curve, not hyperbolic.

front: Distinguish a cofactor from a coenzyme.
back: Both are non-protein helpers. Cofactor = often inorganic (metal ions like Zn2+, Mg2+, Fe2+). Coenzyme = organic, often vitamin-derived (NAD+, FAD, CoA).

front: What is the Gibbs free energy equation, and what sign of ΔG indicates a spontaneous (exergonic) reaction?
back: ΔG = ΔH - TΔS. ΔG < 0 = spontaneous/exergonic; ΔG > 0 = nonspontaneous/endergonic; ΔG = 0 = equilibrium.

front: How does an enzyme affect ΔG and activation energy (Ea) of a reaction?
back: Enzymes lower Ea (speed up rate) but do NOT change ΔG or Keq. Catalysts cannot make a nonspontaneous reaction spontaneous.

front: What are the net products (per glucose) of glycolysis?
back: 2 pyruvate, 2 ATP (net; 4 made - 2 used), and 2 NADH.

front: Which enzyme is the rate-limiting / committed step of glycolysis, and how is it regulated?
back: Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1). Inhibited by ATP and citrate; activated by AMP and fructose-2,6-bisphosphate.

front: What does the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex produce from pyruvate, and what is released?
back: Pyruvate + CoA + NAD+ -> acetyl-CoA + NADH + CO2. Irreversible, occurs in mitochondrial matrix; requires 5 cofactors (TPP, lipoic acid, CoA, FAD, NAD+).

front: Per turn of the TCA (Krebs) cycle, how many NADH, FADH2, and GTP/ATP are produced?
back: 3 NADH, 1 FADH2, 1 GTP (ATP), and 2 CO2 released. Per glucose (2 turns): 6 NADH, 2 FADH2, 2 GTP.

front: What is the final electron acceptor of the electron transport chain, and what does it form?
back: Oxygen (O2) is the final electron acceptor; it combines with electrons and H+ to form water (H2O).

front: How many ATP do NADH and FADH2 each yield via oxidative phosphorylation (standard MCAT values)?
back: NADH ~ 2.5 ATP (older texts ~3); FADH2 ~ 1.5 ATP (older texts ~2). FADH2 yields less because it enters at Complex II, bypassing Complex I.

front: What is the approximate total net ATP yield per glucose from complete aerobic respiration?
back: ~30-32 ATP (older texts cite 36-38). Variation depends on the shuttle (malate-aspartate vs glycerol-phosphate) and P/O ratios used.

front: How does the chemiosmotic gradient drive ATP synthesis in oxidative phosphorylation?
back: ETC pumps H+ into the intermembrane space, creating a proton-motive force; H+ flow back through ATP synthase (Complex V) drives ADP + Pi -> ATP.

front: In DNA, which base pairs form 3 hydrogen bonds vs 2?
back: G-C form 3 H-bonds (stronger, higher Tm); A-T form 2 H-bonds. Higher GC content = more stable, higher melting temperature.

front: Which enzyme synthesizes the RNA primer needed to start DNA replication?
back: Primase (an RNA polymerase). DNA polymerase cannot initiate de novo and requires a free 3'-OH to extend from.

front: In what direction does DNA polymerase synthesize, and which strand is made discontinuously?
back: Synthesizes 5'→3' (reads template 3'→5'). The lagging strand is made discontinuously as Okazaki fragments; the leading strand is continuous.

front: What enzyme joins Okazaki fragments on the lagging strand?
back: DNA ligase forms the phosphodiester bond sealing nicks between fragments after RNA primers are replaced with DNA (by DNA pol I in prokaryotes).

front: Which DNA polymerase activity provides proofreading, and in which direction?
back: 3'→5' exonuclease activity removes mismatched nucleotides during replication, greatly increasing fidelity.

front: What enzyme relieves supercoiling ahead of the replication fork?
back: Topoisomerase (type II = DNA gyrase in prokaryotes) cuts and rejoins DNA strands to relieve torsional strain ahead of helicase.

front: What is the role of RNA polymerase in transcription and does it need a primer?
back: RNA polymerase synthesizes RNA 5'→3' from a DNA template; it does NOT require a primer and lacks proofreading exonuclease activity.

front: Name the three eukaryotic post-transcriptional mRNA modifications.
back: 5' 7-methylguanosine cap, 3' poly-A tail, and splicing out of introns (exons retained) by the spliceosome.

front: What are the start and stop codons in the genetic code?
back: Start: AUG (codes Methionine). Stops: UAA, UAG, UGA (no amino acid; release factors terminate translation).

front: What does it mean that the genetic code is degenerate, and what is wobble?
back: Degenerate = multiple codons code one amino acid. Wobble: the 3rd codon position pairs flexibly, so one tRNA can read several codons.

front: Which ribosomal sites do tRNAs occupy during translation elongation?
back: A (aminoacyl, incoming tRNA), P (peptidyl, holds growing chain), E (exit, deacylated tRNA leaves). Peptide bond forms via peptidyl transferase (rRNA).

front: Contrast missense, nonsense, and silent point mutations.
back: Missense: codon changes the amino acid. Nonsense: codon becomes a stop (truncated protein). Silent: codon changes but same amino acid (degeneracy).

front: Why are frameshift mutations usually more damaging than point substitutions?
back: Insertions/deletions not in multiples of 3 shift the reading frame, altering every downstream codon and often creating premature stop codons.

front: In a monohybrid cross of two heterozygotes (Aa x Aa), what are the genotypic and phenotypic ratios?
back: Genotypic 1:2:1 (AA:Aa:aa); phenotypic 3:1 (dominant:recessive) for complete dominance.

front: State Mendel's law of independent assortment.
back: Alleles of different (unlinked) genes segregate independently during gamete formation. A dihybrid cross (AaBb x AaBb) gives a 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio.

front: What key event distinguishes meiosis I from mitosis, and what is the ploidy outcome?
back: Meiosis I: homologs pair (synapsis), cross over, and separate; it is reductional (diploid→haploid). Mitosis separates sister chromatids and keeps ploidy.

front: When and where does recombination (crossing over) occur in meiosis?
back: During prophase I at chiasmata between homologous chromosomes (in the tetrad, joined by the synaptonemal complex), increasing genetic diversity.

front: Write the two Hardy-Weinberg equations and name a condition that must hold.
back: p+q=1 and p²+2pq+q²=1. Requires: no mutation, no migration, no selection, random mating, large population (no genetic drift).

front: Which cell cycle checkpoint is the main 'restriction point' and what does it check?
back: The G1/S checkpoint checks cell size, nutrients, growth signals, and DNA integrity before committing to replication. p53 acts here.

front: Contrast oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes in cancer.
back: Oncogenes: gain-of-function (e.g., Ras), one mutated allele drives proliferation. Tumor suppressors (e.g., p53, Rb): loss-of-function, typically need both alleles inactivated.

front: Which organelle is the site of the citric acid cycle and oxidative phosphorylation, and what makes it semi-autonomous?
back: Mitochondria. Semi-autonomous: own circular DNA, 70S ribosomes, and self-replicate by binary fission. Inner membrane folds (cristae) hold the electron transport chain; matrix holds TCA enzymes.

front: What is the function of the rough ER versus the smooth ER?
back: Rough ER: protein synthesis/folding (studded with ribosomes), makes secretory/membrane proteins. Smooth ER: lipid & steroid synthesis, detoxification, and Ca2+ storage.

front: What does the Golgi apparatus do, and what is the direction of vesicle flow through it?
back: Modifies, sorts, and packages proteins/lipids (e.g., glycosylation). Flow: cis face (receives from ER) to trans face (ships to membrane/lysosomes/secretion).

front: Lysosomes vs peroxisomes: what does each break down?
back: Lysosomes: acid hydrolases (pH ~5) digest macromolecules and perform autophagy. Peroxisomes: beta-oxidize very-long-chain fatty acids and break down H2O2 via catalase.

front: By Fick's law, what factors determine the rate of simple diffusion across a membrane?
back: Rate rises with the concentration gradient, membrane permeability, and surface area, and falls with membrane thickness. Small, nonpolar molecules diffuse fastest.

front: How does facilitated diffusion differ from active transport in terms of energy and gradient?
back: Facilitated diffusion: passive, uses carrier/channel proteins, moves down the gradient, no ATP. Active transport: moves against the gradient, requires energy (primary = ATP; secondary = ion gradient).

front: In which direction does water move during osmosis, and what is a hypertonic solution's effect on a cell?
back: Water moves from low solute (high water) to high solute (low water). In a hypertonic solution, water leaves the cell, causing it to shrink (crenate).

front: What ratio of ions does the Na+/K+-ATPase pump per ATP, and in which directions?
back: Pumps 3 Na+ OUT and 2 K+ IN per ATP hydrolyzed. It's electrogenic (net + charge out), helping set the resting membrane potential and the Na+ gradient for secondary active transport.

front: According to the fluid mosaic model, how does cholesterol affect membrane fluidity?
back: Cholesterol is a fluidity buffer: at high temps it decreases fluidity (restrains phospholipids); at low temps it increases fluidity (prevents tight packing). Keeps the membrane stable.

front: Which cytoskeletal filament is the thickest, and what are microtubules made of plus their role in mitosis?
back: Microtubules are thickest (~25 nm), made of alpha/beta-tubulin dimers. They form the mitotic spindle, cilia/flagella (9+2), and tracks for kinesin/dynein motor proteins.

front: What protein makes up microfilaments, and what is their primary function?
back: Actin (thinnest, ~7 nm). Functions: muscle contraction (with myosin), cytokinesis (contractile ring), cell motility, and maintaining cell shape.

front: Match each cell junction to its function: tight junction, desmosome, gap junction.
back: Tight junction: seals cells, blocks paracellular leakage. Desmosome: anchors cells for mechanical strength via intermediate filaments. Gap junction: channels for direct cytoplasmic communication.

front: Name three structural features that prokaryotes lack compared to eukaryotes.
back: Prokaryotes lack a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles (no mitochondria/ER/Golgi), and have 70S ribosomes (vs eukaryotic 80S). DNA is a single circular chromosome.

front: What distinguishes Gram-positive from Gram-negative bacteria in cell wall structure?
back: Gram-positive: thick peptidoglycan layer, stains purple. Gram-negative: thin peptidoglycan plus an outer membrane with LPS (endotoxin), stains pink/red.

front: What are the three main shapes of bacteria by morphology?
back: Cocci (spherical), bacilli (rod-shaped), and spirilla/spirochetes (spiral/helical). Can occur in chains (strepto-) or clusters (staphylo-).

front: Describe the four phases of the bacterial growth curve in order.
back: Lag (adaptation, little division), log/exponential (rapid division), stationary (division = death, nutrient depletion), death/decline (death exceeds division).

front: Name the three mechanisms of bacterial genetic recombination (horizontal gene transfer).
back: Transformation (uptake of naked DNA), transduction (DNA transferred by a bacteriophage), and conjugation (direct transfer via sex pilus, often F plasmid).

front: What is the difference between the lytic and lysogenic cycles of a bacteriophage?
back: Lytic: virus replicates immediately and lyses (kills) the host. Lysogenic: viral DNA integrates as a prophage and replicates with host DNA dormantly until induced into the lytic cycle.

front: What enzymes does a retrovirus use to convert its RNA genome into integrated host DNA?
back: Reverse transcriptase converts RNA into double-stranded DNA; integrase inserts that DNA (provirus) into the host genome. Example: HIV.

front: Do viruses meet the criteria for living organisms, and what is their basic structure?
back: No; they are acellular, obligate intracellular parasites (no metabolism, can't reproduce alone). Structure: nucleic acid (DNA or RNA) inside a protein capsid; some have a lipid envelope.

front: What ion gradient and channel maintain the neuron resting membrane potential of about -70 mV?
back: High intracellular K+ leaking out through K+ leak channels sets it (membrane is most permeable to K+); the Na+/K+ ATPase (3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in) maintains the gradients. Potential sits near K+'s equilibrium potential.

front: During an action potential, which channels open first to cause depolarization, and which cause repolarization?
back: Voltage-gated Na+ channels open first (Na+ in = depolarization toward ~+30 to +40 mV); then voltage-gated K+ channels open (K+ out = repolarization). Delayed K+ closure causes the hyperpolarization undershoot.

front: What causes the absolute refractory period of a neuron?
back: Voltage-gated Na+ channels are inactivated (inactivation gate closed) and cannot reopen until the membrane repolarizes, so no new action potential can fire regardless of stimulus strength.

front: How does myelination speed action potential conduction?
back: Saltatory conduction: the impulse regenerates only at nodes of Ranvier, where voltage-gated Na+ channels are concentrated, appearing to jump node-to-node. Myelin insulates internodes, raising conduction velocity.

front: What triggers neurotransmitter release at the presynaptic axon terminal?
back: Depolarization opens voltage-gated Ca2+ channels; Ca2+ influx triggers synaptic vesicle fusion with the presynaptic membrane, releasing neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft by exocytosis.

front: Which neurotransmitter is released at the neuromuscular junction, and what enzyme degrades it?
back: Acetylcholine (ACh) is released; acetylcholinesterase (AChE) in the synaptic cleft breaks it into acetate and choline to terminate the signal.

front: Which division of the autonomic nervous system uses norepinephrine at its target organs, and what is its overall effect?
back: Sympathetic (fight-or-flight): most postganglionic neurons release norepinephrine. Effects: increased HR, bronchodilation, pupil dilation, decreased digestion. (Sweat glands are a cholinergic exception.)

front: Source, action, and feedback control of insulin?
back: Source: pancreatic beta cells. Action: lowers blood glucose (promotes cell uptake, glycogenesis, lipogenesis). Feedback: high blood glucose stimulates release; falling glucose shuts it off (negative feedback).

front: Which hormones regulate blood calcium, and in which directions?
back: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) raises blood Ca2+ (bone resorption, kidney reabsorption, activates vitamin D for gut absorption). Calcitonin (thyroid C cells) lowers blood Ca2+.

front: Trace the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis and its negative feedback.
back: Hypothalamus releases TRH, stimulating anterior pituitary to release TSH, which stimulates the thyroid to release T3/T4. T3/T4 feed back to inhibit both TRH and TSH (negative feedback).

front: Which posterior pituitary hormone increases water reabsorption in the kidney, and where does it act?
back: ADH (vasopressin); acts on collecting duct (and distal tubule), inserting aquaporins to increase water reabsorption, concentrating urine. Made in hypothalamus, stored/released by posterior pituitary.

front: What are the three muscle types and their key control/structure features?
back: Skeletal: striated, voluntary, multinucleated. Cardiac: striated, involuntary, intercalated discs, usually single nucleus. Smooth: non-striated, involuntary, single nucleus.

front: Describe the role of Ca2+ and tropomyosin in the sliding filament cross-bridge cycle.
back: Ca2+ binds troponin, shifting tropomyosin off actin's myosin-binding sites. Myosin heads (energized by ATP hydrolysis) bind actin and pull (power stroke), sliding filaments to shorten the sarcomere.

front: Distinguish innate vs adaptive immunity by speed and specificity.
back: Innate: fast, nonspecific, no memory (skin, phagocytes, complement, inflammation). Adaptive: slower, antigen-specific, has memory (B and T lymphocytes).

front: What do helper T cells (CD4+) vs cytotoxic T cells (CD8+) do, and which MHC do they recognize?
back: CD4+ helper T cells recognize antigen on MHC II (on APCs) and activate B cells/other immune cells. CD8+ cytotoxic T cells recognize MHC I and kill infected/cancerous cells.

front: Where are carbohydrates, proteins, and fats chemically digested, and by what key enzymes?
back: Carbs: mouth (salivary amylase), small intestine (pancreatic amylase). Proteins: stomach (pepsin), small intestine (trypsin/chymotrypsin). Fats: small intestine (pancreatic lipase + bile emulsification).

front: In the nephron, where does most reabsorption occur and what is reabsorbed there?
back: Proximal convoluted tubule: bulk reabsorption of glucose, amino acids, ~65% of Na+ and water, and HCO3-. Glucose/AAs are reabsorbed via secondary active transport coupled to Na+.

front: What does aldosterone do in the nephron and what triggers its release?
back: Aldosterone (adrenal cortex) increases Na+ reabsorption and K+ secretion in the distal tubule/collecting duct, raising water retention and BP. Triggered by angiotensin II (RAAS) and high blood K+.

front: How is most CO2 transported in the blood, and what is the chloride shift?
back: ~70% travels as HCO3- (CO2 + H2O <-> H2CO3 <-> H+ + HCO3-, via carbonic anhydrase in RBCs). Chloride shift: HCO3- exits the RBC as Cl- enters, maintaining charge balance.

front: What does the QRS complex represent, and which valves close during isovolumetric contraction?
back: QRS = ventricular depolarization (triggers contraction). At the start of isovolumetric contraction the AV valves close (S1 heart sound); semilunar valves remain closed until ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure.

front: As you move left-to-right across a period, what happens to atomic radius and why?
back: Atomic radius decreases. Effective nuclear charge increases (more protons) while electrons add to the same shell, pulling them inward.

front: Which has higher first ionization energy and why: Na or Cl?
back: Cl. Ionization energy generally increases left-to-right across a period due to greater effective nuclear charge holding valence electrons more tightly.

front: What does the principal quantum number (n) determine, and what are allowed values?
back: n sets the energy level/shell and size; values are positive integers 1, 2, 3, … Higher n = higher energy and larger orbital.

front: What does the magnetic quantum number (ml) specify, and its allowed range?
back: Orbital orientation in space. ml ranges from -l to +l in integer steps (2l+1 values). For l=1 (p), ml = -1, 0, +1.

front: What type of bond forms between two atoms with a large electronegativity difference (>~1.7)?
back: Ionic bond — electrons are transferred. Small differences give nonpolar covalent; intermediate (~0.5-1.7) give polar covalent.

front: Define a Bronsted-Lowry acid and base.
back: Acid = proton (H+) donor; base = proton (H+) acceptor. The conjugate base is what remains after the acid donates H+.

front: What distinguishes a strong acid from a weak acid in water?
back: Strong acids dissociate completely (e.g., HCl, HNO3, H2SO4). Weak acids partially dissociate, establishing equilibrium with a measurable Ka.

front: What is the conjugate base of H2PO4-?
back: HPO4^2- (remove one H+). Conjugate base = acid minus one proton.

front: At 25 C, what is the relationship between pH and pOH?
How are pKa and Ka related, and what does a lower pKa mean?
back: pKa = -log(Ka). Lower pKa = larger Ka = stronger acid (more dissociation).

front: State the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation.
back: pH = pKa + log([A-]/[HA]). When [A-] = [HA], pH = pKa.

front: At what point is a buffer most effective, and what is the useful buffering range?
back: Most effective when pH = pKa ([A-]=[HA]). Useful range is roughly pKa +/- 1 pH unit.

front: At the half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration, what does pH equal?
back: pH = pKa. Exactly half the acid has been neutralized, so [A-] = [HA].

front: For a weak acid titrated with strong base, is the equivalence point pH acidic, neutral, or basic?
back: Basic (pH > 7). The conjugate base produced reacts with water (hydrolysis), generating OH- and raising pH above 7.

front: State the Gibbs free energy equation and what sign of dG indicates spontaneity.
back: dG = dH - TdS. Spontaneous (favorable) when dG < 0; nonspontaneous when dG > 0; at equilibrium dG = 0.

front: A reaction has dH < 0 and dS > 0. Is it spontaneous, and at what temperatures?
back: Spontaneous at all temperatures. With negative dH and positive dS, dG = dH - TdS is always negative.

front: For the rate law rate = k[A]^2, what is the overall order and units of k?
back: Second order overall; k has units M^-1 s^-1 (1/(M*s)).

front: According to the Arrhenius equation, how do temperature and activation energy affect rate constant k?
back: k = A*e^(-Ea/RT). Raising T or lowering Ea (e.g., via catalyst) increases k. Catalysts lower Ea without being consumed.

front: Per Le Chatelier, how does adding more reactant shift an equilibrium?
back: Shifts right (toward products) to consume the added reactant. K is unchanged at constant T (only changing T changes K).

front: In a galvanic cell, where does oxidation occur and what is the sign of that electrode?
back: Oxidation occurs at the anode (negative in galvanic cells). Reduction at cathode (positive). Mnemonic: 'An Ox, Red Cat.'

front: Write the three primary kinematics equations for constant acceleration.
back: v = v0 + at; Δx = v0t + (1/2)at^2; v^2 = v0^2 + 2aΔx. (Δx = displacement, a = constant acceleration, v0 = initial velocity.)

front: For projectile motion (no air resistance), what are the horizontal and vertical accelerations?
back: Horizontal a = 0 (constant vx); vertical a = g = 9.8 m/s^2 downward. Motions are independent; time aloft is set by the vertical motion.

front: State Newton's second law and define net force.
back: ΣF = ma. Net force equals mass times acceleration; acceleration is in the direction of the net force. Units: N = kg·m/s^2.

front: What is the magnitude of kinetic friction, and how does it compare to static friction?
back: f_k = μ_k·N. Max static f_s ≤ μ_s·N. Typically μ_s > μ_k, so it takes more force to start motion than to keep it moving. N = normal force.

front: State the work-energy theorem.
back: W_net = ΔKE = (1/2)mv_f^2 − (1/2)mv_i^2. Net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy.

front: How is work calculated for a constant force, including the angle?
back: W = F·d·cosθ, where θ is the angle between force and displacement. W is zero when F ⊥ d (cos90° = 0).

front: Give the equations for gravitational PE, kinetic energy, and spring PE.
back: PE_grav = mgh; KE = (1/2)mv^2; PE_spring = (1/2)kx^2. (k = spring constant, x = displacement from equilibrium.)

front: State conservation of mechanical energy and when it applies.
back: KE_i + PE_i = KE_f + PE_f, valid when only conservative forces act (no friction/air resistance). With nonconservative forces: W_nc = ΔKE + ΔPE.

front: Define momentum and state conservation of momentum.
back: p = mv. In a closed system with no net external force, total p is conserved: Σp_before = Σp_after. Impulse J = FΔt = Δp.

front: What is conserved in elastic vs. inelastic collisions?
back: Elastic: both momentum AND kinetic energy conserved. Inelastic: only momentum conserved (KE lost). Perfectly inelastic: objects stick together.

front: Define density and pressure (with the fluid pressure-depth relation).
back: ρ = m/V; P = F/A. Gauge pressure in a static fluid: P = ρgh. Absolute P = P_atm + ρgh. Atmospheric pressure ≈ 1.013×10^5 Pa.

front: State Archimedes' principle and the buoyant force equation.
back: F_b = ρ_fluid·V_displaced·g. Buoyant force equals weight of displaced fluid. Object floats if ρ_object < ρ_fluid.

front: Write the continuity equation and Bernoulli's equation for ideal fluid flow.
back: Continuity: A1v1 = A2v2 (volume flow rate Q constant). Bernoulli: P + (1/2)ρv^2 + ρgh = constant. Faster flow → lower pressure.

front: State Poiseuille's law and what most strongly controls flow rate.
back: Q = (πr^4·ΔP)/(8ηL). Flow rate scales with r^4, so radius dominates; halving radius cuts flow 16-fold. η = viscosity, L = length.

front: State Ohm's law and electrical power (three forms).
back: V = IR. Power P = IV = I^2R = V^2/R. (V in volts, I in amps, R in ohms, P in watts.)

front: How do resistors and capacitors add in series vs. parallel?
back: Resistors series: R = R1+R2; parallel: 1/R = 1/R1+1/R2. Capacitors are opposite: parallel C = C1+C2; series 1/C = 1/C1+1/C2.

front: Give the wave speed equation and how speed relates to frequency and wavelength.
back: v = fλ. For a given medium, v is constant; if f increases, λ decreases. Period T = 1/f. (f in Hz, λ in m.)

front: State the Doppler effect: how does observed frequency shift?
back: f_obs = f_source·(v ± v_obs)/(v ∓ v_source). Approach → higher f; recede → lower f. v = wave (sound) speed; use top signs for approach.

front: Define sound intensity level in decibels and the change per 10× intensity.
back: β = 10·log(I/I0), I0 = 10^-12 W/m^2. Each 10× increase in intensity adds 10 dB. Intensity I ∝ 1/r^2 from a point source.

front: Give the thin-lens/mirror equation, magnification, and Snell's law.
back: 1/f = 1/o + 1/i; m = -i/o = h_i/h_o. Snell: n1·sinθ1 = n2·sinθ2. Converging lens/concave mirror: f > 0; real image: i > 0.

front: Rank these functional groups by IUPAC priority for the principal characteristic group (suffix), highest to lowest: alcohol, ketone, carboxylic acid, ester, aldehyde.
back: Carboxylic acid > ester > aldehyde > ketone > alcohol. (Acids/derivatives outrank carbonyls; alcohol lowest of these.)

front: What suffix and locant rules determine the parent chain in IUPAC nomenclature?
back: Choose the longest carbon chain that contains the highest-priority functional group; number the chain to give that group the lowest possible locant.

front: Define a chiral center and the minimum requirement for chirality at a carbon.
back: A stereocenter is a carbon bonded to 4 different groups. A chiral molecule is non-superimposable on its mirror image and lacks an internal mirror plane.

front: How do you assign R vs S configuration?
back: Rank the 4 groups by CIP priority (higher atomic number = higher). Point lowest priority away; 1→2→3 clockwise = R, counterclockwise = S. If lowest faces you, reverse the call.

front: Distinguish enantiomers from diastereomers.
back: Enantiomers: non-superimposable mirror images; ALL stereocenters are opposite. Diastereomers: stereoisomers that are NOT mirror images; some (not all) stereocenters differ.

front: What is a meso compound?
back: A molecule containing stereocenters but possessing an internal plane of symmetry, making it achiral (superimposable on its mirror image) despite the chiral centers.

front: For a molecule with n stereocenters, what is the maximum number of stereoisomers?
back: 2^n. The actual count is lower when meso forms exist, because internal symmetry makes some 'isomers' identical.

front: Compare SN1 vs SN2: rate law, mechanism, and stereochemistry.
back: SN1: 1st order (rate=k[RX]), carbocation intermediate, racemization, favors 3°. SN2: 2nd order (rate=k[RX][Nu]), one concerted step, backside attack giving inversion, favors 1°.

front: Compare E1 vs E2: rate law and key requirement.
back: E1: 1st order (rate=k[RX]), carbocation intermediate, favors 3° substrate, weak base. E2: 2nd order, concerted, requires anti-periplanar H and leaving group, needs a strong base.

front: Which conditions favor SN2 over SN1?
back: SN2: strong/unhindered nucleophile, polar aprotic solvent (DMSO, acetone, DMF), 1° substrate. SN1: weak nucleophile, polar protic solvent, 3° substrate.

front: What is the general mechanism of nucleophilic addition to a carbonyl?
back: Nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon, breaking the C=O pi bond to form a tetrahedral alkoxide intermediate; protonation then yields the alcohol/adduct.

front: What products form when a Grignard reagent (RMgX) reacts with formaldehyde, an aldehyde, and a ketone?
back: Formaldehyde→1° alcohol; other aldehydes→2° alcohol; ketone→3° alcohol. The Grignard R group adds as a carbanion nucleophile (after aqueous workup).

front: What are the oxidation products of a primary vs secondary alcohol?
back: 1° alcohol → aldehyde (mild, PCC) → carboxylic acid (strong, KMnO4 or Jones/K2Cr2O7). 2° alcohol → ketone. 3° alcohols are not oxidized.

front: Which reagents selectively reduce carbonyls: NaBH4 vs LiAlH4?
back: NaBH4: mild, reduces aldehydes and ketones to alcohols only. LiAlH4: strong, reduces those PLUS carboxylic acids, esters, and amides (to alcohols or amines).

front: What is Fischer esterification and its key feature?
back: Acid-catalyzed, reversible condensation of a carboxylic acid + alcohol → ester + water. Driven forward by excess reactant or by removing water.

front: On what property does liquid-liquid extraction separate compounds?
back: Differential solubility/partitioning between two immiscible solvents (aqueous vs organic). Acid-base chemistry shifts a compound between layers by changing its charge.

front: What property does distillation exploit, and when is fractional distillation used?
back: Separates by differences in boiling point. Use fractional distillation when boiling points are close (within ~25°C); simple distillation when they are far apart.

front: In normal-phase column chromatography (polar stationary, nonpolar mobile), which compounds elute first?
back: Less polar compounds elute first; polar compounds bind the stationary phase and elute last. In TLC, a higher Rf means less polar (traveled farther/faster).

front: Identify the IR stretch ranges for O-H, C=O, and N-H.
back: O-H: broad ~3200-3550 cm⁻¹ (carboxylic acid very broad, 2500-3300); C=O: sharp/strong ~1700 cm⁻¹; N-H: ~3300 cm⁻¹ (1-2 peaks for amines).

front: In ¹H NMR, what do chemical shift, integration, and splitting (n+1) each tell you?
back: Shift = electronic environment (more deshielding = higher ppm); integration = relative number of H; splitting follows the n+1 rule (n = neighboring nonequivalent H's).

front: Define absolute threshold.
back: The minimum stimulus intensity that can be detected 50% of the time.

front: What is the difference threshold (just-noticeable difference, JND)?
back: The minimum difference in stimulus intensity needed to detect a change 50% of the time.

front: State Weber's law and what it implies.
back: JND is a constant proportion (k) of the original stimulus: ΔI/I = k. The JND increases as stimulus magnitude increases (proportional, not absolute).

front: In signal detection theory, what are the four response outcomes?
back: Hit (signal present, detected), miss (signal present, not detected), false alarm (signal absent, reported present), correct rejection (signal absent, not reported).

front: In signal detection theory, what does response bias (criterion) refer to?
back: The tendency to report a signal independent of sensitivity; a liberal criterion increases hits and false alarms, a conservative one decreases both.

front: What is sensory transduction?
back: Conversion of a physical or chemical stimulus into an electrical neural signal by sensory receptors.

front: Compare rods and cones in function.
back: Rods: high sensitivity, dim light/night vision, no color, peripheral. Cones: color vision, fine detail/acuity, bright light, concentrated in fovea.

front: Trace the visual pathway from retina to cortex.
back: Photoreceptors → bipolar cells → ganglion cells → optic nerve → optic chiasm → LGN of thalamus → visual (occipital/striate) cortex.

front: What does the trichromatic (Young-Helmholtz) theory of color vision propose?
back: Color perception arises from 3 cone types most sensitive to short (blue), medium (green), and long (red) wavelengths. Explains color mixing.

front: What does the opponent-process theory of color vision propose?
back: Color is perceived via opposing pairs: red-green, blue-yellow, black-white. Explains afterimages and why we can't see reddish-green.

front: What does place theory of hearing explain?
back: Pitch is determined by the location (place) on the basilar membrane that vibrates most; best for high-frequency sounds.

front: What does frequency (temporal) theory of hearing explain?
back: Pitch is determined by the rate (frequency) of basilar membrane vibration matching the sound's frequency; best for low-frequency sounds.

front: Contrast bottom-up and top-down processing.
back: Bottom-up: perception built from raw sensory data (data-driven). Top-down: perception guided by prior knowledge, expectations, and context (concept-driven).

front: What is the Gestalt principle of proximity?
back: Objects that are close together are perceived as a group or unit.

front: What is the Gestalt principle of closure?
back: The mind fills in gaps to perceive a complete, whole object even when parts are missing.

front: What does selective attention (cocktail party effect) describe?
back: Focusing on one stimulus or conversation while filtering out others, yet still detecting personally relevant info (e.g., your name) in unattended channels.

front: Which brain wave dominates each stage: awake-alert, drowsy/relaxed, NREM Stage 1, and Stage 3?
back: Awake-alert: beta. Relaxed/drowsy: alpha. Stage 1: theta. Stage 3 (deep/slow-wave): delta.

front: What EEG and physiological features define REM sleep?
back: Low-amplitude, fast (beta-like) waves resembling wakefulness, rapid eye movements, vivid dreaming, and skeletal muscle atonia (paradoxical sleep).

front: What brain structure and hormone regulate the circadian rhythm?
back: The suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the hypothalamus controls it; melatonin (from the pineal gland) promotes sleepiness and is regulated by light.

front: Which sleep stage shows sleep spindles and K-complexes?
back: NREM Stage 2; spindles and K-complexes appear amid theta waves.

front: In classical conditioning, what is the unconditioned stimulus (UCS)?
back: A stimulus that naturally and automatically triggers a response without prior learning (e.g., food causing salivation).

front: In Pavlov's experiment, after conditioning, what does the bell represent and what response does it produce?
back: The bell is the conditioned stimulus (CS); it produces salivation, the conditioned response (CR).

front: What is the difference between stimulus generalization and stimulus discrimination?
back: Generalization: responding to stimuli similar to the CS. Discrimination: responding to the CS but not to similar stimuli.

front: In operant conditioning, distinguish negative reinforcement from punishment.
back: Negative reinforcement removes an aversive stimulus to INCREASE behavior. Punishment DECREASES behavior (positive=add aversive; negative=remove pleasant).

front: Which reinforcement schedule produces the highest, most consistent response rate and is most resistant to extinction?
back: Variable-ratio (reinforcement after an unpredictable number of responses, e.g., slot machines/gambling).

front: What response pattern does a fixed-interval schedule produce?
back: A scalloped pattern: responding drops just after reinforcement, then increases as the next interval ends (e.g., studying right before a scheduled exam).

front: What is shaping in operant conditioning?
back: Reinforcing successive approximations of a target behavior until the full desired behavior is achieved.

front: What is observational learning, and who is the key theorist (Bobo doll)?
back: Learning by watching and imitating others (modeling); Albert Bandura, demonstrated with the Bobo doll experiment.

front: What are the three stages of memory storage in order?
back: Sensory memory → short-term (working) memory → long-term memory.

front: What is the capacity and duration of short-term memory?
back: About 7 +/- 2 items (Miller's magic number) for roughly 15-30 seconds without rehearsal.

front: Distinguish explicit (declarative) from implicit (procedural) long-term memory.
back: Explicit: conscious facts/events (semantic + episodic). Implicit: unconscious skills/conditioned responses (e.g., riding a bike).

front: What is the difference between semantic and episodic memory?
back: Semantic: general facts and knowledge. Episodic: personally experienced events tied to time/place.

front: What is the difference between recall and recognition retrieval?
back: Recall: retrieving info without cues (fill-in-blank). Recognition: identifying learned info among options (multiple choice). Recognition is easier.

front: What does Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve show?
back: Memory retention drops rapidly soon after learning, then levels off; spaced repetition/relearning slows the decline.

front: What are the three components of an attitude (ABC model)?
back: Affective (feelings), Behavioral (actions/tendencies), and Cognitive (beliefs/thoughts).

front: What is cognitive dissonance and how is it typically resolved?
back: Discomfort from holding conflicting attitudes/behaviors; resolved by changing a belief, changing behavior, or adding justifying cognitions.

front: In the elaboration likelihood model, contrast the central vs. peripheral route to persuasion.
back: Central: deep processing of argument quality (lasting change, high motivation). Peripheral: superficial cues like attractiveness/credibility (temporary change).

front: What did Asch's conformity experiment demonstrate?
back: On a line-judgment task, people conformed to an obviously wrong group majority on ~37% of critical trials, showing normative social influence.

front: What did Milgram's obedience study find regarding shock administration?
back: About 65% of participants delivered the maximum 450-volt shock when ordered by an authority figure.

front: What is the bystander effect and what mechanism explains it?
back: Individuals are less likely to help a victim when others are present, due to diffusion of responsibility (more witnesses = less personal duty).

front: Which sociological paradigm views society as a system of interdependent parts working together to maintain stability and equilibrium?
back: Functionalism (structural functionalism) — society is like an organism; each institution serves a function. Associated with Durkheim and Parsons.

front: Distinguish manifest functions from latent functions (functionalism).
back: Manifest = intended, recognized consequences of an institution. Latent = unintended, unrecognized consequences. Dysfunction = a part that disrupts stability. Terms from Merton.

front: Which paradigm sees society as competition among groups over scarce resources, emphasizing power inequality and class struggle?
back: Conflict theory — derived from Karl Marx (bourgeoisie vs. proletariat). Inequality persists because dominant groups protect their interests.

front: Which micro-level paradigm focuses on how individuals create shared meaning through symbols and face-to-face interaction?
back: Symbolic interactionism — society is built from everyday interactions and the meanings people assign to symbols/language. Associated with Mead and Blumer.

front: What does social constructionism argue about social reality?
back: Reality (e.g., race, gender, money) is created and maintained through shared social agreement, not inherent in nature. Meaning is socially constructed.

front: Name the five major social institutions commonly tested on the MCAT.
back: Family, education, religion, government/economy, and medicine/healthcare. (Some lists also include mass media.)

front: Define socialization, and distinguish primary from secondary socialization.
back: Socialization = lifelong process of learning norms/values/behaviors of one's culture. Primary = childhood (family). Secondary = later, outside the home (school, peers, work).

front: What is the difference between an agent of socialization and resocialization?
back: Agents = sources of socialization (family, peers, school, media). Resocialization = discarding old behaviors for new ones, often in a total institution (prison, military).

front: What are the four stages of the demographic transition model?
back: 1) High birth & death rates (preindustrial). 2) Death rate falls, population booms. 3) Birth rate falls. 4) Low birth & death rates, stable population (industrialized).

front: What is social stratification?
back: The hierarchical arrangement of people into social classes/strata based on unequal access to resources, wealth, power, and prestige.

front: Contrast a caste system with a class system of stratification.
back: Caste = closed, ascribed status fixed at birth, no mobility. Class = open, achieved status possible, allows social mobility.

front: Define intragenerational vs. intergenerational social mobility.
back: Intragenerational = change in social position within one person's lifetime. Intergenerational = change in social position between parents and their children.

front: What is meritocracy, and how does it relate to social mobility?
back: Meritocracy = a system where social position is based on individual ability/effort (merit) rather than ascribed traits, ideally enabling upward mobility.

front: Identify the three types of capital (Bourdieu): social, cultural, economic.
back: Economic = money/assets. Social = networks/connections one can leverage. Cultural = knowledge, skills, education, tastes that confer status.

front: Distinguish embodied, objectified, and institutionalized cultural capital.
back: Embodied = internalized skills/dispositions/accent. Objectified = cultural goods owned (books, art). Institutionalized = credentials/degrees.

front: According to Max Weber, what three dimensions determine social class/standing?
back: Class (economic/wealth), status (prestige/honor), and party (power/political influence). Weber argued stratification is multidimensional, not just economic.

front: Differentiate power and prestige in social stratification.
back: Power = ability to achieve goals despite resistance / impose one's will. Prestige = the respect, esteem, and social honor attached to one's position.

front: What are the social determinants of health (SDOH)?
back: Conditions in which people are born, live, work, and age that affect health — e.g., income, education, housing, employment, food/healthcare access, environment.

front: What is the social gradient in health?
back: A stepwise inverse relationship: as socioeconomic status rises, health outcomes improve at every level — not just rich vs. poor, but graded across the whole hierarchy.

front: Define a health disparity (health inequity).
back: Preventable, unjust differences in health outcomes/burden of disease across populations linked to social, economic, or environmental disadvantage (e.g., race, SES).

front: In Piaget's stages, at what stage and age does object permanence develop?
back: Sensorimotor stage (birth to ~2 years). The infant learns that objects continue to exist even when out of sight.

front: Which Piagetian stage features egocentrism and lack of conservation, and what age range?
back: Preoperational stage (~2-7 years): symbolic thinking and pretend play, but egocentrism and no conservation/reversibility.

front: At which Piaget stage do children master conservation and logical operations on concrete objects?
back: Concrete operational stage (~7-11 years): conservation, reversibility, and logical thought about concrete events (not abstract).

front: Which Piaget stage introduces abstract and hypothetical (deductive) reasoning?
back: Formal operational stage (~11+ years): abstract logic, hypothetical-deductive reasoning, and sophisticated moral reasoning.

front: What is Erikson's psychosocial conflict of adolescence?
back: Identity vs. role confusion (~12-18 yrs): adolescents work to develop a personal identity and a stable sense of self.

front: Name Erikson's stages for young adulthood, middle adulthood, and late adulthood.
back: Young: intimacy vs. isolation; Middle: generativity vs. stagnation; Late: integrity (ego integrity) vs. despair.

front: What are the three levels of Kohlberg's theory of moral development?
back: Preconventional (obedience/self-interest), Conventional (conformity/law & order), Postconventional (social contract/universal ethics).

front: What is the zone of proximal development (ZPD) in Vygotsky's theory?
back: The gap between what a learner can do alone and what they can do with guidance/scaffolding from a more knowledgeable other.

front: What attachment style is shown by a child who is distressed at separation but easily comforted on the caregiver's return?
back: Secure attachment. Reflects a responsive, consistent caregiver; child uses the caregiver as a secure base to explore.

front: Which attachment style shows extreme distress, then both seeking and resisting the caregiver (ambivalence) on reunion?
back: Anxious-ambivalent (resistant) attachment, arising from inconsistent caregiving.

front: What does drive-reduction theory of motivation propose?
back: Physiological needs create internal tension (drives); behavior aims to reduce that tension and return to homeostasis.

front: List the levels of Maslow's hierarchy of needs from bottom to top.
back: Physiological, Safety, Love/belonging, Esteem, Self-actualization (some versions add self-transcendence at the top).

front: What three innate needs does self-determination theory say drive intrinsic motivation?
back: Autonomy, Competence, and Relatedness.

front: Per the James-Lange theory of emotion, what is the order of events?
back: Stimulus leads to physiological/bodily arousal, then to emotion. You feel afraid BECAUSE you tremble (arousal precedes and causes emotion).

front: How does the Cannon-Bard theory of emotion differ from James-Lange?
back: Cannon-Bard: physiological arousal and the conscious emotional experience occur simultaneously and independently in response to a stimulus.

front: What does the Schachter-Singer (two-factor) theory require for an emotion?
back: Physiological arousal PLUS a cognitive label/appraisal of that arousal based on context. Arousal + interpretation = emotion.

front: What are the three stages of Selye's General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS)?
back: Alarm (fight-or-flight activation), Resistance (sustained coping/high cortisol), Exhaustion (resources depleted, illness risk).

front: In a major depressive episode, what is the minimum symptom duration and number of symptoms required?
back: >=5 symptoms for >=2 weeks, at least one of which is depressed mood or anhedonia (loss of interest/pleasure).

front: What distinguishes bipolar I from bipolar II disorder?
back: Bipolar I: at least one full manic episode (~1 week+). Bipolar II: at least one hypomanic episode plus a major depressive episode, with no full mania.

front: Distinguish positive vs. negative symptoms of schizophrenia.
back: Positive (added): hallucinations, delusions, disorganized speech/behavior. Negative (lost): flat affect, avolition, alogia, anhedonia.

front: At physiological pH (7.4), what is the overall charge and ionic form of a typical amino acid (no ionizable side chain)?
back: Net charge 0 — exists as a zwitterion: protonated alpha-amino group (-NH3+) and deprotonated alpha-carboxyl (-COO-).

front: Which standard amino acid has the highest pI (~10.8), and why is it so basic?
back: Arginine — its guanidinium side chain has a very high pKa (~12.5), keeping it positively charged and raising the pI.

front: When the solution pH is below an amino acid's pI, what net charge does it carry and which way does it migrate in electrophoresis?
back: Net positive charge (protonated); migrates toward the cathode (negative electrode).

front: Which amino acid's small hydrogen side chain allows it to fit tight turns and pack closely, e.g., in collagen's Gly-X-Y repeat?
back: Glycine — its side chain is a single H, giving maximal backbone flexibility and letting it fit the crowded core of the collagen triple helix.

front: What hydroxyl-bearing amino acids are the major targets of regulatory phosphorylation by kinases?
back: Serine, threonine (both have -OH), and tyrosine (phenolic -OH). Kinases transfer a phosphate from ATP onto these hydroxyls.

front: In the alpha helix, which backbone atoms hydrogen-bond, and what is the spacing between them?
back: The backbone C=O of residue n bonds to the N-H of residue n+4 (H-bonds run parallel to the helix axis). ~3.6 residues per turn.

front: What does a Ramachandran plot graph, and what do its allowed regions represent?
back: Plots phi (φ) vs psi (ψ) backbone dihedral angles. Allowed regions = sterically permitted conformations; distinct clusters correspond to alpha helices and beta sheets.

front: Why does proline disrupt alpha helices and is rarely found within them?
back: Its side chain locks into the backbone (rigid ring), fixing phi and removing the amide N-H needed for the helix's hydrogen bond. It often kinks/breaks helices.

front: In a parallel vs antiparallel beta sheet, which has more linear (stronger) hydrogen bonds?
back: Antiparallel — H-bonds are straight/linear, making it more stable. Parallel sheets have slanted, weaker H-bonds between adjacent strands.

front: What is the primary driving force behind protein folding into a compact tertiary structure?
back: The hydrophobic effect — nonpolar side chains cluster in the core to minimize contact with water, increasing water's entropy. Polar/charged residues face outward.

front: Which covalent bond uniquely stabilizes tertiary/quaternary structure, where does it form, and between which residues?
back: Disulfide bond (-S-S-) formed by oxidation of two cysteine thiols. Common in extracellular/secreted proteins (e.g., antibodies, insulin).

front: What do molecular chaperones (e.g., the chaperonins/Hsp family) do, and do they alter the final folded structure?
back: They prevent improper aggregation and provide an isolated environment for correct folding (often ATP-dependent). They do NOT change the final native structure dictated by the sequence.

front: What did Anfinsen's ribonuclease experiment demonstrate about protein folding?
back: Primary structure (amino acid sequence) alone determines the native 3D conformation — denatured RNase spontaneously refolded to full activity once denaturant was removed.

front: Contrast fibrous vs globular proteins in solubility, structure, and function (with examples).
back: Fibrous: elongated, repetitive secondary structure, insoluble, structural (collagen, keratin). Globular: compact, folded, water-soluble, functional (enzymes, hemoglobin).

front: What gives collagen its tensile strength at the structural level?
back: A right-handed triple helix of three left-handed polypeptide strands, with a repeating Gly-X-Y sequence (X often proline, Y often hydroxyproline) stabilized by interchain H-bonds.

front: Why does scurvy weaken collagen, biochemically?
back: Vitamin C (ascorbate) is a cofactor for prolyl/lysyl hydroxylase; without it, proline/lysine hydroxylation fails, destabilizing the collagen triple helix.

front: How does the O2-binding curve of myoglobin differ from hemoglobin, and why?
back: Myoglobin: hyperbolic (single subunit, high affinity, no cooperativity). Hemoglobin: sigmoidal (4 subunits, cooperative binding). Myoglobin stores O2 in muscle.

front: Define cooperativity in hemoglobin and the two conformational states involved.
back: O2 binding to one subunit increases affinity of the others (positive cooperativity), shifting from the low-affinity T (tense) state to the high-affinity R (relaxed) state.

front: What is the Bohr effect on hemoglobin's oxygen affinity?
back: Increased CO2 and decreased pH (more H+) lower Hb's O2 affinity, shifting the curve right — promoting O2 release in active, metabolizing tissues.

front: How does 2,3-BPG (2,3-bisphosphoglycerate) affect hemoglobin, and where does it bind?
back: It binds in the central cavity of deoxy-Hb (stabilizing the T state), lowering O2 affinity and shifting the curve right — aids O2 unloading, e.g., at altitude.

front: Hemoglobin and myoglobin are allosteric vs non-allosteric — which is which, and what does that mean?
back: Hemoglobin is allosteric (effectors like H+, CO2, 2,3-BPG bind at distinct sites to modulate O2 affinity). Myoglobin is non-allosteric (single subunit, no cooperative regulation).

front: On a titration curve of an amino acid, what does each pKa correspond to and what region indicates buffering?
back: Each pKa is an inflection/midpoint where [protonated]=[deprotonated] (50% titrated). The flat plateaus around each pKa are the buffering regions (max buffer at pH = pKa).

front: What is the transition state of a reaction, and how does an enzyme stabilize it?
back: The highest-energy point (peak of the energy diagram) along the reaction path; it is NOT an isolable intermediate. Enzymes bind/stabilize the transition state, lowering Ea.

front: Contrast the lock-and-key vs induced-fit models of enzyme-substrate binding.
back: Lock-and-key: active site already complements substrate. Induced-fit: substrate binding causes a conformational change in the enzyme to optimize fit (favored model).

front: What does kcat (turnover number) represent?
back: Number of substrate molecules converted to product per enzyme active site per unit time when the enzyme is saturated. kcat = Vmax/[E]total.

front: What does the specificity constant kcat/Km measure, and what is its upper limit?
back: Catalytic efficiency; ranks an enzyme's preference among substrates. Diffusion-limited (catalytically perfect) enzymes approach ~10^8-10^9 M^-1 s^-1.

front: On a Lineweaver-Burk plot, how does a competitive inhibitor change the lines vs no inhibitor?
back: Lines share the y-intercept (same Vmax); the inhibitor line has a steeper slope and a less negative x-intercept (-1/Km), reflecting an increased apparent Km.

front: On a Lineweaver-Burk plot, where do lines intersect for a pure noncompetitive inhibitor?
back: On the x-axis (same x-intercept = unchanged Km); the inhibited line has a higher y-intercept (lower Vmax) and steeper slope.

front: On a Lineweaver-Burk plot, what is characteristic of uncompetitive inhibition?
back: Parallel lines (unchanged slope = Km/Vmax, since both fall proportionally). The inhibited line shifts up: higher y-intercept (lower Vmax) and decreased Km.

front: Why must an uncompetitive inhibitor bind only the enzyme-substrate (ES) complex?
back: It binds a site that exists only after substrate binds; with no free enzyme to bind, it lowers both Km (Le Chatelier pulls toward ES) and Vmax.

front: Contrast the Monod (concerted/MWC) and Koshland (sequential) models of cooperativity.
back: MWC: all subunits switch together between T and R states (no hybrids). Sequential: each subunit's binding induces stepwise changes, allowing mixed-state intermediates.

front: Define a homotropic vs heterotropic allosteric effector.
back: Homotropic: the substrate itself acts as the regulator (positive cooperativity). Heterotropic: a different molecule (activator or inhibitor) binds an allosteric site.

front: How does reversible phosphorylation regulate enzymes, and what enzymes add/remove the phosphate?
back: Phosphate (from ATP) added by kinases, removed by phosphatases. Can activate or inhibit; alters charge/conformation. A key fast, reversible covalent modification.

front: What is a zymogen, and give two digestive examples.
back: An inactive enzyme precursor activated by irreversible proteolytic cleavage. Examples: trypsinogen->trypsin, pepsinogen->pepsin (also chymotrypsinogen).

front: Which active coenzymes are derived from vitamins B1, B2, B3, and B5?
back: B1 (thiamine)->TPP; B2 (riboflavin)->FAD/FMN; B3 (niacin)->NAD+/NADP+; B5 (pantothenate)->coenzyme A.

front: What coenzymes come from vitamins B6, B7, B9, and B12?
back: B6->PLP (pyridoxal phosphate; transaminations); B7 (biotin)->carboxylase cofactor; B9 (folate)->THF (1-carbon transfer); B12 (cobalamin)->methyl-/adenosylcobalamin.

front: Relate ΔG°' to the equilibrium constant Keq.
back: ΔG°' = -RT ln(Keq). Keq > 1 gives negative ΔG°' (favorable); Keq < 1 gives positive ΔG°'. At equilibrium ΔG = 0.

front: How does ATP hydrolysis drive an endergonic reaction (energetic coupling)?
back: A shared intermediate couples the reactions; the large negative ΔG of ATP->ADP+Pi (~-7.3 kcal/mol standard) sums with the positive ΔG so the total is negative.

front: What are the three irreversible bypass steps unique to gluconeogenesis?
back: Pyruvate carboxylase + PEP carboxykinase (pyruvate->PEP), fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase, and glucose-6-phosphatase. They bypass glycolysis's irreversible steps.

front: What are the two main products and the purpose of the oxidative phase of the pentose phosphate pathway (PPP)?
back: Produces NADPH (for biosynthesis/antioxidant defense) and ribose-5-phosphate (for nucleotides). Rate-limiting enzyme: glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase.

front: Which enzymes break down and build glycogen, and what activates each via phosphorylation?
back: Glycogen phosphorylase degrades; glycogen synthase builds. Phosphorylation (via PKA, e.g. from glucagon/epinephrine) activates phosphorylase but inactivates synthase.

front: At which ETC complexes are protons pumped, and which complex does NOT pump protons?
back: Complexes I, III, and IV pump H+ into the intermembrane space. Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase) transfers electrons but pumps no protons.

front: What does each cycle of fatty-acid beta-oxidation yield, and where does it occur?
back: Each round yields 1 FADH2, 1 NADH, and 1 acetyl-CoA, shortening the chain by 2 carbons. Beta-oxidation occurs in the mitochondrial matrix.

front: What is the committed, rate-limiting step of fatty-acid synthesis, and where does it occur?
back: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase converts acetyl-CoA to malonyl-CoA (uses biotin, CO2, ATP) in the cytoplasm; activated by citrate, inhibited by palmitoyl-CoA.

front: What is the function of single-strand binding proteins (SSBs) during DNA replication?
back: They bind and coat separated single-stranded DNA at the replication fork to prevent re-annealing and protect it from degradation and secondary structure (hairpin) formation.

front: How do telomeres and telomerase solve the end-replication problem?
back: DNA pol can't fully replicate the lagging strand's 5' end (RNA primer gap); telomerase, a reverse transcriptase with its own RNA template, extends the 3' end with TTAGGG repeats.

front: Mismatch repair vs nucleotide excision repair: which fixes thymine dimers from UV light?
back: Nucleotide excision repair (NER) removes bulky lesions like UV-induced pyrimidine dimers; mismatch repair corrects base-pairing errors that escape proofreading.

front: What conserved DNA promoter element in eukaryotes is bound by TBP/TFIID, and roughly where is it?
back: The TATA box, located ~25-35 bp upstream (around -25) of the transcription start site; bound by TATA-binding protein (TBP) within TFIID.

front: Which eukaryotic RNA polymerase transcribes mRNA (protein-coding genes)?
back: RNA polymerase II. (Pol I makes most rRNA; Pol III makes tRNA and 5S rRNA.)

front: What modified nucleotide forms the 5' cap of eukaryotic mRNA and what does it do?
back: 7-methylguanosine (m7G) added via a 5'-5' triphosphate linkage; protects from exonucleases and aids ribosome binding and nuclear export.

front: In splicing, which sequences mark the intron, and what is the catalytic complex?
back: Introns begin GU at the 5' splice site and end AG at the 3' site, with an internal branch-point A; the spliceosome (snRNPs) catalyzes removal via a lariat intermediate.

front: What is alternative splicing and why is it significant?
back: Differential inclusion of exons from one pre-mRNA, letting a single gene produce multiple protein isoforms; expands proteome diversity beyond gene number.

front: Which factor delivers aminoacyl-tRNA to the ribosomal A site during elongation (eukaryotes/prokaryotes)?
back: Elongation factor: eEF1A in eukaryotes (EF-Tu in prokaryotes), using GTP hydrolysis to load charged tRNA into the A site.

front: What catalyzes peptide bond formation during translation, and what type of catalyst is it?
back: Peptidyl transferase, an activity of the large-subunit rRNA, making the ribosome a ribozyme (RNA-based catalysis).

front: How does a signal sequence direct co-translational protein targeting?
back: An N-terminal signal peptide is recognized by SRP, which pauses translation and docks the ribosome to the RER for co-translational import into the ER lumen.

front: Name three common post-translational modifications and one functional role of each.
back: Phosphorylation (activity/signaling switch), glycosylation (folding/cell recognition), ubiquitination (tags protein for proteasomal degradation).

front: In the lac operon, what happens to transcription when lactose is present but glucose is absent?
back: Allolactose inactivates the repressor (operon ON) and low glucose raises cAMP, so CAP-cAMP binds and strongly activates transcription—maximal expression.

front: Contrast the lac (inducible) and trp (repressible) operons.
back: lac is inducible: normally off, turned on by an inducer (allolactose). trp is repressible: normally on, turned off when corepressor (tryptophan) activates the repressor.

front: What are the two main epigenetic mechanisms, and which generally silences genes?
back: DNA methylation (CpG, typically silencing) and histone modification (acetylation loosens chromatin to activate; deacetylation/some methylation can silence).

front: What is genomic imprinting?
back: Parent-of-origin epigenetic silencing so only the maternal or paternal allele is expressed; e.g., Prader-Willi (paternal allele lost) and Angelman (maternal allele lost) syndromes.

front: Distinguish incomplete dominance from codominance.
back: Incomplete dominance: heterozygote shows a blended intermediate phenotype (red x white = pink). Codominance: both alleles fully and separately expressed (AB blood type).

front: How is a classic X-linked recessive trait expressed differently in males vs females?
back: Males (XY) are hemizygous—one recessive allele causes the trait; females need two copies. Affected males inherit it from carrier mothers; no male-to-male transmission.

front: How does recombination frequency relate to gene distance on a chromosome?
back: Recombination frequency rises with distance up to a 50% maximum; 1% recombination = 1 map unit (centimorgan). Genes recombining at 50% are effectively unlinked.

front: In Hardy-Weinberg, if recessive disease incidence (q²) is 1/10,000, what is the carrier frequency?
What is the role of the denaturation, annealing, and extension steps in PCR?
back: Denaturation (~95°C) separates strands; annealing (~50-65°C) binds primers; extension (~72°C) lets Taq polymerase synthesize new DNA. Cycles amplify exponentially.

front: What does each blot detect: Southern, Northern, Western?
back: Southern = DNA, Northern = RNA, Western = protein (detected with antibodies). Mnemonic: SNoW DRoP (DNA, RNA, Protein).

front: Which organelle modifies and tags proteins with mannose-6-phosphate, and what is the fate of proteins bearing this tag?
back: The cis-Golgi adds mannose-6-phosphate; this tag binds M6P receptors in the trans-Golgi network, directing the proteins to lysosomes.

front: What signal sequence directs a ribosome to the rough ER during co-translational translocation?
back: An N-terminal hydrophobic signal peptide, recognized by the signal recognition particle (SRP), which docks the ribosome to the SRP receptor on the rough ER.

front: Which coat protein mediates retrograde (Golgi-to-ER) vesicle transport versus ER-to-Golgi anterograde transport?
back: COPI mediates retrograde Golgi-to-ER transport; COPII mediates anterograde ER-to-Golgi transport.

front: What is the role of clathrin in membrane trafficking?
back: Clathrin forms the coat for receptor-mediated endocytosis and vesicle budding from the trans-Golgi/plasma membrane, forming clathrin-coated pits and vesicles.

front: In Michaelis-Menten kinetics of a carrier-mediated transporter, what does Vmax represent and why does it plateau?
back: Vmax is the maximum transport rate when all carriers are saturated with substrate; the plateau reflects a finite number of transporter proteins (saturation kinetics).

front: Distinguish a symporter, antiporter, and uniporter in secondary active transport.
back: Symporter moves two solutes the same direction; antiporter moves two solutes in opposite directions; uniporter moves one solute. Sym/antiport use an ion gradient for energy.

front: In GPCR signaling, what does the Gs alpha subunit activate, and what second messenger results?
back: Gs-alpha activates adenylyl cyclase, converting ATP to cyclic AMP (cAMP), which activates protein kinase A (PKA).

front: In the phospholipase C pathway, what two second messengers are produced from PIP2 and what does each do?
back: PLC cleaves PIP2 into IP3 (releases Ca2+ from the ER) and DAG (activates protein kinase C, PKC).

front: How does a receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) activate upon ligand binding?
back: Ligand binding causes receptor dimerization and autophosphorylation of tyrosine residues, creating docking sites that trigger the Ras/MAPK cascade.

front: Which enzyme terminates cAMP signaling, and what drug class inhibits it?
back: Phosphodiesterase (PDE) degrades cAMP to AMP; PDE inhibitors (e.g., caffeine, sildenafil) prolong second-messenger signaling.

front: At which cell cycle checkpoint does the cell verify DNA is undamaged before committing to replication, and what is its other name?
back: The G1/S checkpoint (the restriction point); it checks for DNA damage, cell size, and growth signals before entering S phase.

front: What complex must form to drive the transition from G1 to S phase, and what inhibits it?
back: Cyclin-CDK complexes (e.g., cyclin D-CDK4/6) drive progression; CDK inhibitors like p21 (activated by p53) and p16 block them.

front: What is checked at the G2/M checkpoint, and which complex triggers mitotic entry?
back: G2/M checks for complete, undamaged DNA replication; the cyclin B-CDK1 complex (maturation/mitosis-promoting factor, MPF) triggers entry into mitosis.

front: In intrinsic-pathway apoptosis, what does mitochondrial cytochrome c release trigger?
back: Cytochrome c forms the apoptosome with Apaf-1, activating initiator caspase-9, which activates executioner caspases (e.g., caspase-3) to dismantle the cell.

front: Which motor protein moves cargo toward microtubule plus-ends versus minus-ends?
back: Kinesin moves toward the plus-end (typically anterograde, away from the nucleus); dynein moves toward the minus-end (retrograde, toward the nucleus).

front: What motor protein and filament generate force in skeletal muscle contraction?
back: Myosin (motor) walks along actin microfilaments, hydrolyzing ATP to produce the power stroke of the sliding-filament mechanism.

front: What is the 9+2 microtubule arrangement, and which structures have it versus 9+0?
back: Motile cilia/flagella have a 9+2 axoneme (9 doublets + 2 central singlets); centrioles and basal bodies have a 9+0 triplet arrangement.

front: Which cell junction anchors cells to the extracellular matrix/basement membrane, and what cytoskeletal element connects to it?
back: Hemidesmosomes anchor cells to the basement membrane via integrins; they connect to intermediate filaments (keratin) inside the cell.

front: What is the bacterial F (fertility) plasmid required for, and what does its presence designate the cell?
back: The F plasmid encodes the sex (F) pilus needed for conjugation; an F+ cell is the donor that transfers a copy of F to an F- recipient.

front: Bacteria are haploid with a single circular chromosome; what mode of reproduction produces clones, and how is its rate often expressed?
back: Binary fission (asexual) produces genetically identical clones; growth rate is expressed as generation/doubling time during exponential phase.

front: What distinguishes a (+) sense from a (-) sense single-stranded RNA virus upon entering a host cell?
back: (+)ssRNA can be directly translated as mRNA by host ribosomes; (-)ssRNA must first be transcribed to (+) mRNA by viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase carried in the virion.

front: What is the eclipse period in the viral lytic cycle?
back: The interval after host-cell entry/uncoating during which no complete infectious virions are detectable, while viral components are being synthesized before assembly and release.

front: What ion and channel cause the repolarization (falling) phase of a neuron action potential?
back: K+ efflux through voltage-gated potassium channels, which open slowly and drive Vm back toward EK (~ -90 mV). Their delayed closing causes the brief after-hyperpolarization.

front: What is the approximate threshold and peak voltage of a typical neuronal action potential?
back: Threshold ~ -55 mV; peak ~ +30 to +40 mV. Once threshold is reached, the all-or-none spike fires.

front: In excitation-contraction coupling, what protein on the T-tubule senses depolarization and what does it trigger?
back: The dihydropyridine (DHP) receptor (voltage sensor) on the T-tubule mechanically activates the ryanodine receptor on the sarcoplasmic reticulum, releasing Ca2+ into the cytosol.

front: What pumps Ca2+ back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum to end muscle contraction, and what powers it?
back: The SERCA pump actively transports Ca2+ into the SR using ATP; lowering cytosolic Ca2+ lets troponin/tropomyosin re-block actin, causing relaxation.

front: What distinguishes an EPSP from an IPSP at a chemical synapse?
back: EPSP: depolarizing (e.g., Na+ influx) brings neuron toward threshold; IPSP: hyperpolarizing (e.g., Cl- influx or K+ efflux) moves it away. Summation determines firing.

front: Differentiate temporal vs spatial summation at a neuron.
back: Temporal: rapid repeated inputs from ONE presynaptic neuron add up over time. Spatial: simultaneous inputs from MULTIPLE neurons add together. Both push toward threshold.

front: In a monosynaptic stretch (myotatic) reflex, how many synapses are between sensory and motor neuron, and give the classic example.
back: One synapse directly between the afferent sensory neuron and efferent motor neuron. Example: knee-jerk (patellar) reflex from muscle spindle stretch.

front: List the five components of a reflex arc in order.
back: Receptor to afferent (sensory) neuron to integration center (spinal cord/CNS) to efferent (motor) neuron to effector (muscle/gland).

front: Contrast the somatic and autonomic motor pathways by number of neurons and neurotransmitter at the effector.
back: Somatic: one neuron, releases ACh onto skeletal muscle. Autonomic: two neurons (pre- and postganglionic); effector gets ACh (parasympathetic) or NE (most sympathetic).

front: What neurotransmitter and receptor type act at ALL autonomic ganglia (both sympathetic and parasympathetic)?
back: Acetylcholine acting on nicotinic receptors at the preganglionic-to-postganglionic synapse in autonomic ganglia.

front: Trace the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis from hypothalamus to product.
back: Hypothalamus releases CRH to anterior pituitary, which releases ACTH, stimulating the adrenal cortex (zona fasciculata) to release cortisol; cortisol feeds back negatively.

front: What does the complement membrane attack complex (MAC) do, and which proteins form it?
back: C5b-C6-C7-C8-C9 assemble into a pore in the pathogen membrane, causing osmotic lysis. Multiple C9 molecules polymerize to form the channel.

front: Which complement fragment is the major opsonin and which are anaphylatoxins?
back: C3b is the main opsonin (tags pathogens for phagocytosis). C3a, C4a, and C5a are anaphylatoxins that trigger inflammation; C5a is also a potent chemotactic agent.

front: Which antibody isotype is the first produced in a primary immune response, and which is most abundant overall?
back: IgM is produced first (pentamer, strong complement activator). IgG is the most abundant in serum and the main player in secondary responses; it crosses the placenta.

front: Which antibody mediates allergic/anti-parasite responses, and which is found in mucosal secretions?
back: IgE binds mast cells/basophils, triggering histamine release in allergy and parasite defense. IgA (secretory dimer) protects mucosal surfaces and is in breast milk, saliva, and tears.

front: Which kidney transporter drives reabsorption in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, and is it permeable to water?
back: The Na+/K+/2Cl- (NKCC2) cotransporter reabsorbs salt; the thick ascending limb is impermeable to water, so it dilutes the filtrate (the 'diluting segment').

front: How does ADH alter water permeability in the collecting duct at the molecular level?
back: ADH binds V2 receptors, raising cAMP, which inserts aquaporin-2 channels into the apical membrane, allowing water reabsorption and concentrating urine.

front: Write the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for the bicarbonate buffer system and the normal blood pH.
